headings.text: S C H U O N
L i f e a n d T e a c h i n g s
SUNY series in Western Esoteric Traditions
STATE UNIVERSITY OF NEW YORK PRESS
I dissolve and blossom in the perfume of thy soul.
Schuon in Paris, 1932. Personal collection, Catherine Schuon.
Da kam—ein wacher Traum—die Jungfrau süß,
A bouquet of flowers had been brought to me—
Then came—a waking dream—the Virgin sweet,
Ich wollte dieses Buch schon lang beschliessen—
Lasst uns dem Ruf des Höchsten Folge leisten—
I could not do so; I had to go on writing poems.
For there are other preoccupations, other duties;
Be that as it may, whatever we may wish to do:
Let us be obedient to the call of the Most High.
All my thoughts speak of love.
Autumn leaves—what does this image mean?
Poems arrive late to my ear,
I know not how—their source is Light and Love—
Tutti li miei penser parlan d’Amore.
And he gave ear unto me.
In the day of my trouble I sought the Lord:
My sore ran in the night and ceased not:
My soul refused to be comforted.
I remembered God and was troubled.
With permission of the Schuon Estate.
Bibliography of Works by Frithjof Schuon
